part_i_general_contact_information___app.relationship_to_organization_ie_program_: Volunteer Program Director and Head Coach

part_i_general_contact_information___tea.type_of_program: Community-based, after-school youth sports program run in partnership with Cleveland Metropolitan School District middle schools
part_i_general_contact_information___tea.gender_of_participants: Co-ed (boys and girls practicing and learning together, with plans for separate game squads as numbers grow)
part_i_general_contact_information___tea.level_of_participants: Middle school youth, grades 6–8, almost all first-time and beginner players
part_i_general_contact_information___tea.equipment_grant_package_if_awarded: Starter team package for 30 middle school players: boys’ and girls’ sticks, helmets, gloves, arm pads, shoulder pads, goggles, pinnies, and 2 full goalie kits, appropriate for grades 6–8, plus a small set of extra sticks for PE and introductory clinics.

part_ii_history__development.why_and_in_what_year_was_the_team_establ:
Our team, East Side Cleveland Youth Lacrosse, was officially established in 2023, but the idea started taking shape in late 2021. I (Michael Thompson) grew up in the Cleveland area, played club lacrosse in college, and then moved back to the east side after graduating. I noticed that while some of the suburban schools had well-established lacrosse programs, many of the city schools—especially on the east side—had no access to the sport at all.

In the fall of 2021, I was volunteering with an after-school homework help and basketball program at a local middle school. During one of the sessions, I brought in my old lacrosse stick just to toss the ball around outside with a couple of students while we waited for other kids to arrive. Within about ten minutes, I had a small crowd of curious sixth and seventh graders asking what kind of stick it was, whether it was "like hockey," and if they could try it. A few of them picked it up surprisingly quickly, but what really stuck with me was how excited they were to be trying something they had literally never seen before in their own neighborhood.

Over the winter of 2021–2022, I started talking with the school’s assistant principal, the PE teacher, and a few parents about whether there might be interest in a more formal lacrosse program. They all said the same thing: there was definitely interest, but absolutely no equipment and very limited budget. Most of our students’ families do not have the resources to buy specialized sports gear, and the school’s athletic funds are already stretched thin covering uniforms and transportation for basketball and track.

In early 2022, I organized a few no-contact intro clinics using my own equipment and a handful of donated sticks from former teammates. We ran three Saturday sessions in a small grass lot behind the school. We had about 15 kids show up consistently, despite the fact that we could only offer sticks and a couple of soft balls. Several students and parents asked repeatedly, "When are we going to have a real team?" and "Will we ever get to play other schools?" That was the point where I realized this needed to be more than a short-term clinic.

Throughout the rest of 2022, I met with representatives from two other east side middle schools and a local community center that already runs a flag football and summer basketball league. We discussed the possibility of creating a shared community-based lacrosse program that could feed into existing high school teams or even help new ones start up. The biggest barrier identified in every conversation was equipment: sticks, helmets, pads, and goalie gear are significantly more expensive than a basketball or a soccer ball, and there is currently no dedicated funding stream for a new sport.

In January 2023, with support from the East Side Youth Sports Collaborative (a small nonprofit that helps coordinate youth sports programming in our area), we formally established "East Side Cleveland Youth Lacrosse" as a community-based program serving multiple middle schools. Our goal from the beginning has been to:
1. Introduce lacrosse to students in neighborhoods that have historically had little to no access to the sport.
2. Provide all necessary equipment at no cost to participants.
3. Emphasize academic support, teamwork, and leadership development alongside athletic skills.
4. Build a sustainable pathway so interested players can continue into high school programs.

We chose 2023 as our official founding year because that is when we first secured a consistent practice field (at a shared city park), established a regular practice schedule (twice per week after school), obtained basic liability coverage through our partner nonprofit, and recruited a small volunteer coaching staff. In spring 2023, we piloted a very informal version of the program with 18 students from two middle schools. We practiced using a mix of worn-out donated gear and a limited number of shared sticks. We focused on fundamentals—cradling, passing, catching, footwork, and basic offensive and defensive concepts—without playing full-contact scrimmages because we simply didn’t have enough properly fitting protective equipment.

Despite these limitations, the response from students and families was overwhelmingly positive. Parents told us that their kids were coming home talking about lacrosse nonstop, looking up professional and college highlights on YouTube, and even trying to practice cradling in the house using rolled-up socks. One parent mentioned that her son, who had previously struggled with attendance and motivation, started making a point of getting to school on time on practice days so he wouldn’t risk being benched.

By the fall of 2023, word of mouth had spread to a third middle school and a neighborhood recreation center. We held an interest meeting and were surprised when over 40 students signed up to learn more, even though we were very transparent about our equipment challenges. Ultimately, we had to cap our initial spring 2024 roster at 28 participants because that was the maximum number we felt we could safely accommodate with the limited sticks and protective gear we had been able to piece together from donations.

The main reason for formally establishing the team in 2023 was to create a structured, consistent program that could serve as a foundation for growth. Instead of one-off clinics, we now have:
- A set weekly practice schedule (Mondays and Wednesdays, 3:30–5:00 p.m.)
- A defined coaching staff (myself as head coach/program director, plus two assistant coaches—one is a former women’s college player, and the other is a high school teacher who played club lacrosse)
- A partnership with the East Side Youth Sports Collaborative to help with insurance, background checks, and transportation coordination
- Support from three middle schools that encourage their students to participate and allow us to distribute flyers and speak at assemblies

However, we are still in the early stages of development, and lack of equipment remains the single biggest barrier to fully realizing our vision for the program. Right now, most of our players share sticks, and some practices involve rotating gear between drills. We have only a few helmets and sets of pads, which means we cannot safely run full small-sided scrimmages or schedule formal games against other teams. We also do not have proper goalie equipment, so our "goalies" are currently just shooting targets or cones.

The long-term vision, which we began articulating when we established the team in 2023, is to:
- Build two full co-ed middle school teams (roughly 20–25 players each) who can safely practice and scrimmage in a game-like environment
- Introduce age-appropriate versions of the boys’ and girls’ rule sets so that players understand the differences and can transition smoothly into high school programs
- Host at least one small jamboree or play day with another First Stick or nearby youth program within the next two years
- Work with local high school coaches so that our eighth graders have a clear pathway to continue playing

The year 2023 is therefore both our official founding year and the point at which our informal interest and trial clinics evolved into a structured, multi-school community program. The team was established because we saw a clear and urgent need to expand access to lacrosse beyond the suburbs and into city neighborhoods where students rarely, if ever, see this sport represented. Many of our kids have told us directly that they had never heard of lacrosse before our program, but now they talk about wanting to play in high school and even dream about college opportunities.

In short, East Side Cleveland Youth Lacrosse was founded in 2023 to close an access gap, provide a positive and structured after-school option in a community with limited resources, and use lacrosse as a vehicle for mentorship, physical fitness, and academic encouragement. The First Stick program aligns almost perfectly with the vision we had when we started: building a sustainable, standards-based, and inclusive lacrosse home for middle school students on Cleveland’s east side who otherwise would not have the chance to pick up a stick at all.
